content.text: llama.cpp is a pure C++ inference engine for transformer models. It introduced the GGUF file format, which stores model weights alongside tokenizer vocabulary and metadata in a single portable binary. Because it uses hand-written SIMD kernels (AVX2, NEON) and pluggable GPU backends (Metal, CUDA), it runs efficiently on hardware ranging from a Raspberry Pi to an M3 Max to a multi-GPU workstation — with no Python or PyTorch required at the C++ level.

attrs.alt: llama.cpp architecture: GGUF file → llama.cpp core (ggml tensor engine, KV cache, sampling) → language bindings (llama-cpp-python, LangChain, CLI, llama-server) with hardware backends (CPU/Metal/CUDA) and GGUF quantization levels

content.text: GGUF (GGML Universal Format) is the successor to the original GGML binary format. A GGUF file contains everything needed for inference:
: magic bytes, version, tensor count, metadata count.
: architecture name, context length, embedding dimension, attention heads, rope scaling, BOS/EOS token IDs, and all tokenizer vocabulary/merges.
: name, shape, type (quant level), and byte offset into the data section.
: raw quantized weight bytes, memory-mapped at runtime for zero-copy loading.
Memory-mapping means llama.cpp can start generating tokens seconds after launch — it doesn't need to copy all weights into RAM before inference begins. The OS pages weights in on demand.
GGUF files come in different quantization levels that trade model size and RAM against output quality. The naming scheme
Default for most use cases; best balance
When Q4 accuracy is marginal on your task
compiles the C++ library on install. Set environment variables to enable GPU acceleration before installing.

# CPU-only (universal, works everywhere)
pip install llama-cpp-python

# Apple Silicon (Metal GPU acceleration)
CMAKE_ARGS="-DGGML_METAL=on" pip install llama-cpp-python --force-reinstall

# NVIDIA GPU (CUDA acceleration)
CMAKE_ARGS="-DGGML_CUDA=on" pip install llama-cpp-python --force-reinstall

# Verify: loads the shared library and reports backend
python -c "from llama_cpp import Llama; print('OK')"

from llama_cpp import Llama

llm = Llama(
    model_path="./models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf",
    n_ctx=4096,          # context window (tokens)
    n_gpu_layers=-1,     # -1 = offload all layers to GPU; 0 = CPU only
    n_threads=8,         # CPU threads for non-GPU computation
    verbose=False,       # suppress progress output
)

# Raw completion (not chat format)
output = llm(
    "The capital of France is",
    max_tokens=64,
    temperature=0.0,
    stop=["\n"],
)
print(output["choices"][0]["text"])
# → " Paris."

# Chat completion (uses the model's chat template)
response = llm.create_chat_completion(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain GGUF in one sentence."},
    ],
    max_tokens=100,
    temperature=0.7,
)
print(response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])

from llama_cpp import Llama

llm = Llama(model_path="./models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf", n_gpu_layers=-1)

stream = llm.create_chat_completion(
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Count to 5."}],
    stream=True,
    max_tokens=64,
)

for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk["choices"][0]["delta"]
    text = delta.get("content", "")
    if text:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
print()
parameter controls how many transformer layers run on the GPU. The remaining layers run on the CPU. Set it to
to offload everything (if VRAM is sufficient) or to a positive integer if you need to split across CPU and GPU.

from llama_cpp import Llama

# A 7B Q4_K_M model has ~32 transformer layers.
# If you have 6 GB VRAM: offload 28 of 32 layers → 4 run on CPU.
llm_partial = Llama(
    model_path="./models/llama-3.1-7b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf",
    n_gpu_layers=28,
    n_ctx=2048,
)

# If you have 8+ GB VRAM: offload all layers
llm_full = Llama(
    model_path="./models/llama-3.1-7b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf",
    n_gpu_layers=-1,
    n_ctx=4096,
)

# Check which backend was selected
print(llm_full.metadata)  # includes backend info and n_gpu_layers used
llama.cpp exposes a richer set of sampling controls than most APIs:
Nucleus sampling; keep tokens covering top_p of probability mass
Keep only the top K tokens at each step
Minimum probability relative to top token; removes very unlikely tokens
Penalise recently generated tokens; reduces repetition loops
Alternative to top-p/top-k; targets a perplexity level dynamically

from llama_cpp import Llama

# embedding=True puts the model in embedding mode (no causal LM head)
embedder = Llama(
    model_path="./models/nomic-embed-text-v1.5.Q4_K_M.gguf",
    embedding=True,
    n_gpu_layers=-1,
    n_ctx=512,
    verbose=False,
)

texts = [
    "llama.cpp runs on CPU and GPU",
    "GGUF is the model file format for llama.cpp",
    "Python is a dynamically typed language",
]

# Returns list of float vectors
vectors = [embedder.embed(t) for t in texts]
print(f"Embedding dim: {len(vectors[0])}")  # e.g., 768

# Cosine similarity
import math

def cosine(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
    na = math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in a))
    nb = math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in b))
    return dot / (na * nb)

print(cosine(vectors[0], vectors[1]))  # ~0.93 (related)
print(cosine(vectors[0], vectors[2]))  # ~0.78 (less related)
llama.cpp ships a built-in HTTP server (formerly
) that exposes an OpenAI-compatible API. It also bundles a simple web UI for interactive testing.

# Build from source (macOS / Linux)
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp && cd llama.cpp
cmake -B build -DGGML_METAL=on   # or -DGGML_CUDA=on
cmake --build build -j --target llama-server

# Start the server
./build/bin/llama-server \
    --model models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf \
    --host 127.0.0.1 \
    --port 8080 \
    --ctx-size 4096 \
    --n-gpu-layers 99   # -1 not valid for the CLI; use a large number

# Test from Python using the OpenAI SDK
python3 -c "
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url='http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1', api_key='no-key')
r = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='llama-3.2-3b-instruct',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello!'}],
)
print(r.choices[0].message.content)
"
wrapper for direct in-process integration, or point

# pip install langchain-community llama-cpp-python
from langchain_community.llms import LlamaCpp
from langchain_core.callbacks import StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate

llm = LlamaCpp(
    model_path="./models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf",
    n_gpu_layers=-1,
    n_ctx=2048,
    temperature=0.0,
    max_tokens=256,
    callbacks=[StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler()],  # stream to stdout
    verbose=False,
)

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Summarise the following in one sentence:\n\n{text}"
)
chain = prompt | llm
result = chain.invoke({"text": "GGUF is a binary format that packs model weights and tokenizer into a single file for llama.cpp."})

# Alternatively, use the server with ChatOpenAI (no LlamaCpp import needed)
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm_server = ChatOpenAI(
    model="llama-3.2-3b",
    base_url="http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1",
    api_key="not-required",
)

from huggingface_hub import hf_hub_download
import os

def download_gguf(repo_id: str, filename: str, local_dir: str = "./models") -> str:
    os.makedirs(local_dir, exist_ok=True)
    path = hf_hub_download(
        repo_id=repo_id,
        filename=filename,
        local_dir=local_dir,
    )
    print(f"Downloaded to {path}")
    return path


# Common GGUF model repositories
models = [
    # repo_id                                          filename
    ("bartowski/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-GGUF",         "Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf"),
    ("bartowski/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-GGUF",   "Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf"),
    ("nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5-GGUF",          "nomic-embed-text-v1.5.Q4_K_M.gguf"),
]

# Download the 3B model for quick experiments
path = download_gguf(*models[0])

llama.cpp can constrain token sampling to a BNF-like grammar, guaranteeing that the output is valid JSON (or any other format) without post-processing or retry loops. This is done at the token sampling layer, not by post-filtering.

from llama_cpp import Llama, LlamaGrammar

JSON_GRAMMAR = r"""
root   ::= object
value  ::= object | array | string | number | ("true" | "false" | "null") ws

object ::= "{" ws (string ":" ws value ("," ws string ":" ws value)*)? "}" ws
array  ::= "[" ws (value ("," ws value)*)? "]" ws
string ::= "\"" ([^\\"\x7F\x00-\x1F] | "\\" ["\\/bfnrt] | "\\u" [0-9a-fA-F]{4})* "\"" ws
number ::= "-"? ("0" | [1-9][0-9]*) ("." [0-9]+)? ([eE] [-+]? [0-9]+)? ws
ws     ::= ([ \t\n] ws)?
"""

llm = Llama(
    model_path="./models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf",
    n_gpu_layers=-1,
    verbose=False,
)

grammar = LlamaGrammar.from_string(JSON_GRAMMAR)

output = llm(
    'Return JSON with keys "name" and "year" for the creator of Python.',
    grammar=grammar,
    max_tokens=64,
    temperature=0.0,
)
print(output["choices"][0]["text"])
# → {"name": "Guido van Rossum", "year": 1991}
If a model is not yet on HuggingFace in GGUF format, convert it using llama.cpp's bundled conversion script.

# 1. Download the HuggingFace model
pip install huggingface_hub
python3 -c "
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download
snapshot_download('meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct', local_dir='./hf-model')
"

# 2. Convert to F16 GGUF
python3 llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./hf-model --outfile ./models/llama-3.2-3b-f16.gguf

# 3. Quantize to Q4_K_M
./build/bin/llama-quantize \
    ./models/llama-3.2-3b-f16.gguf \
    ./models/llama-3.2-3b-q4_k_m.gguf \
    Q4_K_M
without GPU flags builds a CPU-only binary. If you add a GPU later, reinstall with

is the maximum token window. Setting it too high allocates excess RAM for the KV cache even when requests are short. Setting it too low causes truncation on long inputs. Match it to your typical request size.
, you must manually format the prompt with the model's
template. Missing this causes poor instruction-following.
: When offloading only some layers, the remaining CPU layers can bottleneck throughput. It's often faster to use a smaller fully-GPU model than to split a larger one.
: Complex grammars can slow sampling by 10–30% at each token step. For simple JSON, the built-in
